perturbation.original_text: Late payments of undisputed amounts shall accrue interest at a rate equal to two percent (2%) per annum, until paid. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL within fifteen (15) Days of the invoice date or resolution of any dispute, if applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: Late payments of undisputed amounts shall accrue interest at a rate equal to two percent (2%) per annum, until paid. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL within thirty (30) Days of the invoice date or resolution of any dispute, if applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment period to 30 days could violate Ohio's Prompt Payment Law for construction contracts, which specifies payment timelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 4113.61 outlines the requirements for prompt payment to subcontractors, material suppliers, or laborers by contractors and owners. While this contract is not explicitly a construction contract, if it involves construction-related services, a 30-day payment term might violate the prompt payment provisions if the services fall under the scope of the statute and a shorter payment period is legally mandated. The altered timeline contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of Ohio's prompt payment laws, particularly if the underlying services relate to improvements on the pipeline.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 4113.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 4113.61 | Time limitations for payments to subcontractors and materialmen. Ohio Revised Code / Title 41 Labor and Industry / Chapter 4113 Miscellaneous Labor Provisions Effective: September 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 153 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A)(1) If a subcontractor or material supplier submits an application or request for payment or an invoice for materials to a contractor in sufficient time to allow the contractor to include the application, request, or invoice in the contractor's own pay request submitted to an owner, the contractor, within ten calendar days after receipt of payment from the owner for improvements to property, shall pay to the: (a) Subcontractor, an amount that is equal to the percentage of completion of the subcontractor's contract allowed by the owner for the amount of labor or work performed; (b) Material supplier, an amount that is equal to all or that portion of the invoice for materials which represents the materials furnished by the material supplier. The contractor may reduce the amount paid by any retainage provision contained in the contract, invoice, or purchase order between the contractor and the subcontractor or material supplier, and may withhold amounts that may be necessary to resolve disputed liens or claims involving the work or labor performed or material furnished by the subcontractor or material supplier. If the contractor fails to comply with division (A)(1) of this section, the contractor shall pay the subcontractor or material supplier, in addition to the payment due, interest in the amount of eighteen per cent per annum of the payment due, beginning on the
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period to 30 days, which could contradict Ohio Revised Code § 4113.61 if the contract involves construction-related services and a shorter payment period is legally mandated. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that contractors have ten calendar days after receiving payment from the owner to pay subcontractors or material suppliers. The change from 15 to 30 days could violate this prompt payment provision, creating a potential legal contradiction as per the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: MPL will provide a thirty (30) day notice to Shipper, notifying it of the actual in-service date for the included projects.
perturbation.changed_text: MPL will provide a fifteen (15) day notice to Shipper, notifying it of the actual in-service date for the included projects.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for the in-service date to 15 days may contradict standard industry practices or regulatory requirements for providing adequate notice, especially if longer periods are expected or required by regulatory agencies like the FERC.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 341.3 outlines the procedures for changing rates on oil pipelines. While this section does not explicitly dictate the notice period for the 'in-service date' of a project, it emphasizes the requirement for just and reasonable rates and practices. A reduced notice period might not allow shippers adequate time to assess the impact of the in-service date on tariffs and potentially challenge them, thus conflicting with the FERC's regulatory oversight ensuring fair practices. The regulatory body usually expects a 30-day minimum.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 341.3 - Form of tariff. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 341.3 - Form of tariff. CFR prev | next § 341.3 Form of tariff. (a) Tariffs may be filed either by dividing the tariff into tariff sections or as an entire document. (b) Contents of tariff. All tariff publications must contain the following information in the following order: (1) Title page. The title page of each tariff must contain the following information: (i) The FERC tariff number designation, in the upper right hand corner, numbered consecutively, and the FERC tariff number designation of the tariff that is canceled, if any, under it; (ii) The corporate name of the carrier; (iii) The type of rates, e.g., local, joint, or proportional, and the commodity to which the tariff applies, e.g., crude, petroleum product, or jet fuel; (iv) Governing tariffs, e.g., separate “rules and regulations” tariffs, if any; (v) The specific Commission order pursuant to which the tariff is issued; (vi) The issue date, which must be shown on the lower left side, and the effective date, which must be shown on the lower right side; (vii) The expiration date, if applicable; (viii) The name of the issuing officer or duly appointed official issuing the tariff, the complete street and mailing address of the carrier, and the name and phone number of the individual responsible for compiling the tariff publication. (2) Table of contents. Tariffs of more than nine pages in length must contain a table of contents. A table of contents is optional for tariffs which are less than 10 pages in length. (3) A list of carriers participating in joint tariffs. (4) Index of Commodities. (5) Explanatory statements. These statements must explain the proper application of rates and rules. (6) Rules governing tariff publications. (i) All rules affecting the rates or the services provided for in the tariff publication must be included. A spe

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period from 30 days to 15 days, potentially conflicting with the principle of 'just and reasonable rates and practices' as emphasized in 18 CFR § 341.3. While the snippet does not explicitly mention the 30-day requirement, the law explanation argues that a reduced notice period might not allow shippers adequate time to assess tariff impacts, potentially conflicting with FERC's oversight role, and the regulatory body expects a 30-day minimum. The original text aligns with this expectation, while the changed text deviates from it, creating a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Shipper must notify MPL if it is unable to execute a reasonably acceptable commodity supply agreement within one hundred and eighty (180) days of the Project in-service date.
perturbation.changed_text: Shipper must notify MPL if it is unable to execute a reasonably acceptable commodity supply agreement within thirty (30) days of the Project in-service date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period to 30 days for the commodity supply agreement may unreasonably limit the shipper's time to secure such an agreement, potentially contradicting principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contract law, as well as potentially violating regulations requiring reasonable timeframes for such actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code. While this transportation agreement may not be fully governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith is a general contract law concept. Reducing the notification period from 180 to 30 days could be seen as a violation of good faith if it does not allow the shipper a reasonable opportunity to secure a commodity supply agreement, which the contract acknowledges as important. This is a contradiction of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing that is part of contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period from 180 to 30 days, which could be considered a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it doesn't allow the shipper a reasonable opportunity to secure a commodity supply agreement. This aligns with the definition of inconsistencies legal contradiction, where time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.", indicating a legal requirement for good faith in contract performance, which is potentially undermined by the reduced notification period.
